packet.criterion_text: Objective severity trend: PASI, BSA, and/or IGA show a directional change across visits.

evidence.quote: Severity scoring today: PASI 6.1, BSA 8%, IGA 2
PASI 6.3 today (6.1 on 2026-02-02 — essentially flat, within measurement noise). BSA 8%. IGA 2.
Few scattered erythematous papules on the antecubital arm, limited extent
Confluent erythematous plaques with overlying scale over the arm, extensive

packet.reasoning: PASI rose from 6.1 (2026-02-02) and 6.3 (2026-03-16) to 14.2 (2026-05-10) and 16.8 (2026-06-29), a rise far exceeding the 2-point threshold; BSA rose from 8% to 18% and IGA stepped up from 2 to 3. The photographic progression from limited papules to extensive confluent plaques corroborates the objective worsening.

packet.criterion_text: Symptom trajectory, including nail and joint involvement, shows a directional change.

evidence.quote: onycholysis noted on two fingernails (right index and right middle), with distal separation and slight yellowish discoloration
Nail plate pitting with mild subungual discoloration and ridging
Erythematous scaly patch with scale along the frontal scalp hairline, localized
Thick adherent scale over erythematous frontal scalp plaques, moderate to extensive
Grouped erythematous papules and small plaques over the arm, mild to moderate extent
Confluent erythematous plaques with overlying scale over the arm, extensive

packet.reasoning: New nail involvement (onycholysis on two fingernails plus photographed pitting) appears on 2026-05-10, and photographs document scalp disease at 2026-05-10 that progresses from localized to moderate-to-extensive by 2026-06-29, alongside arm lesions progressing from mild-to-moderate papules to extensive confluent plaques. Together these show a worsening symptom trajectory including new nail and scalp sites. No joint symptoms are documented in the candidates.
packet.gap: No visit after May 10 re-examined or re-documented the fingernail changes that were first noted then.

packet.criterion_text: DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index) trend shows a directional change.

evidence.quote: DLQI 7, driven by residual itch and self-consciousness at the gym.
DLQI 15 today (was 8), with high scores on symptoms, feelings, and work domains.
DLQI 17. Compared with 2026-05-10 (PASI 14.2, DLQI 15), objective and quality-of-life measures are both worse.

packet.reasoning: DLQI rose from 7 (2026-02-02) and 8 (2026-03-16) to 15 (2026-05-10) and 17 (2026-06-29). The jump from 8 to 15 exceeds the 4-point threshold for meaningful worsening, and the subsequent rise to 17 confirms a sustained upward trend.

packet.criterion_text: Treatment-response adequacy: the current regimen has been given an adequate, adherent trial.

evidence.quote: Adherence and pharmacy fill detail deferred to today's separate review by clinic staff (filed separately).

packet.reasoning: The only candidate speaking to adherence states that adherence and pharmacy fill details were deferred to a separately filed review that is not among the provided evidence. Without the regimen's start date, dosing schedule, or any record confirming doses were taken as scheduled, the adequacy of the treatment trial cannot be assessed from the quoted evidence.
packet.gap: The June 29 visit deferred the adherence and pharmacy-fill review to a separate document that is not in the chart provided.

packet.criterion_text: Worsening cannot be attributed to disease if a confounder (non-adherence, infection, steroid-withdrawal or seasonal flare) is unaddressed.
packet.reasoning: No candidates were returned for this criterion: the provided evidence contains no documentation screening for or excluding non-adherence, intercurrent infection, steroid taper/withdrawal, or seasonal flare. Because the candidates are empty and do not speak to the criterion, the confounder question cannot be assembled from quoted evidence. The best-case attribution — that the objective worsening documented under criteria 1-3 reflects disease activity — remains unverified against the confounder classes.
packet.gap: None of the visits documents whether missed medications, an infection, a steroid taper, or a seasonal pattern was considered and ruled out as the cause of the flare.

attempts: confounder missed doses infection steroid seasonal
